title: Method of screening synthetic promoters
abstract: The ability to pyramid traits in a single crop is critical for current strategies aimed at crop improvement, however, the precise regulation of transgene expression in the host can be a major challenge. Synthetic promoters provide a solution to problems associated with homology dependent gene silencing events, however few plant systems exist which allow the large scale screening of promoter candidates. The present invention provides a high-throughput method for the isolation and transformation of plant protoplasts which permits such a high throughput screen to be carried out.
claims:
Claims A high-throughput method of screening one or more promoters for their ability to drive expression of an operably linked reporter nucleic acid in a plant protoplast, the method comprising the steps of; (i) providing a population of isolated plant protoplasts; (ii) transforming said protoplasts with one or more heterologous polynucleotides, which heterologous polynucleotides comprise a promoter operably linked to a reporter nucleic acid; and (iii) measuring the expression of the reporter nucleic acid that is expressed under the control of said promoter in one or more protoplasts. A method as claimed in claim 1 , wherein the steps of the method are performed in an automated system. A method as claimed in claim 1 or claim 2, wherein the protoplasts are isolated from Zea mays; preferably leaves of Zea mays. A method as claimed in any one of claims 1 to 3, wherein the reporter nucleic acid comprises a reporter gene which encodes a reporter protein capable of giving an optical signal corresponding to its abundance. A method as claimed in claim 4, wherein the reporter protein is luciferase. A method as claimed in any preceding claim wherein the isolation and transformation of protoplasts, the method comprising the steps of: (a) isolating tissue containing cells of interest from a plant (preferably from one or more seedlings, e.g. zea mays seedlings); (b) incubating the tissue in an isolation buffer comprising cellulase and macerozyme; (c) filtering the resulting solution to remove undigested tissue material; (d) centrifuging the filtrate of step (c) to pellet protoplasts present in the filtrate and removing the supernatant; (e) optionally, washing the protoplasts; (f) re-suspending the protoplasts in a transformation buffer; (g) transforming the protoplasts with one or more polynucleotides; and (h) incubating the transformed protoplasts with an incubation buffer. 7. A method as claimed in claim 6 wherein the seedlings are Zea mays seedlings, and preferably the seedlings are etiolated. 8. A method as claimed in claim 7, wherein the Zea mays seedlings are 1 1 days old. 9. A method as claimed in any of claims 6 to 8 wherein the cellulase is cellulase R10. 10. A method as claimed in any of claims 6 to 9 wherein the macerozyme is macerozyme R10. 1 1. A method as claimed in any one of claims 6 to 10, wherein the cellulase and macerozyme are pre-heated to 50-65 횂째C prior incubation with the tissue. A method as claimed in any of claims 6 to 1 1 , wherein step (b) comprises incubating the mesophyll cells with cellulase and macerozyme under vacuum. A method as claimed in of claims 6 to 12, wherein washing the protoplasts in step (e), re-suspension of protoplasts in step (f), and/or moving the transformed protoplasts to a the incubation buffer of step (h) comprises using a multi-step buffer addition process. 14. A method as claimed in of claims 6 to 13, wherein during washing the protoplasts in step (e), and/or moving the transformed protoplasts to a the incubation buffer of step (h), surfaces of utilised vessels are pre-treated with a protein-containing composition to reduce or prevent adhesion of the protoplasts to the vessel surface. 15. A method as claimed in any of claims 6 to 14, wherein in step (d) the filtrate is centrifuged at 150g or less. 16. A method as claimed in any of claims 6 to 15, wherein protoplast transformation with one or more polynucleotides is achieved by electroporation. 17. A method as claimed in any of claims any of claims 6 to 16, wherein the protoplasts are electroporated at an electroporation voltage of from 330 to 370 V/cm. 18. A method as claimed in any of claims 6 to 17, wherein at least 667 protoplasts per 횓쩌횓혲 are electroporated; optionally at least 1000 per 횓쩌횓혲. 19. A method as claimed in any of claims 6 to 18, wherein the incubation buffer comprises bovine serum albumin (BSA); optionally 0.5% (w/v) BSA. 20. A method as claimed in any of claims 6 to 19, wherein step (h) of the method comprises incubating the protoplasts in darkness; optionally for a period of 18 hours. 21. A method as claimed in any of claims 6 to 20 wherein after step (h) the protoplasts are lysed and then expression of the reporter nucleic acid is measured, preferably based upon fluorescence or luminescence. 22. A method as claimed in any preceding claim wherein the isolation and transformation of protoplasts, the method comprises the steps of: (a) isolating tissue containing mesophyll cells from the leaves of etiolated zea mays seedlings; (b) incubating the mesophyll cells in an isolation buffer comprising cellulase R10 enzyme and macerozyme R10, preferably for at least 3 hours; (c) filtering the resulting solution to remove undigested tissue material; (d) centrifuging the filtered solution of step (c) and removing the supernatant; (e) washing the protoplasts thereby obtained using a multi-step buffer transfer process wherein a protein composition is applied to prevent protoplast adhesion to the vessel in which washing occurs; (f) re-suspending the protoplasts in a transformation buffer (preferably comprising Mannitol, MES and KCI) using a multi-step buffer addition process; (g) transforming the protoplasts with one or more heterologous polynucleotides by electroporation; (h) transferring the transformed protoplasts to an incubation buffer (preferably comprising Mannitol, MES, KCI and bovine serum albumin (BSA)) using a multi- step buffer transfer process; (i) incubating the transformed protoplasts in the incubation buffer; and (j) measuring expression of the reporter nucleic acid. 23. A method of obtaining a population of protoplasts suitable for a high throughput screening assay, the method comprising: (a) isolating tissue containing mesophyll cells from the leaves of etiolated seedlings; (b) incubating the mesophyll cells in an isolation buffer comprising cellulase R10 enzyme and macerozyme, preferably for at least 3 hours; (c) filtering the resulting solution to remove undigested tissue material; 

 (d) centrifuging the filtered solution of step (c) and removing the supernatant; and (e) washing the protoplasts thereby obtained using a multi-step buffer transfer process wherein a protein composition is applied to prevent protoplast adhesion to the vessel in which washing occurs. 24. A method as claimed in claim 23 wherein the macerozyme is macerozyme R10. 25. A method as claimed in claim 23 or 24 wherein, during washing the protoplasts in step (e), surfaces of utilised vessels are pre-treated with a protein-containing composition to reduce or prevent adhesion of the protoplasts to the vessel surface. 26. A population of plant protoplasts obtainable by the method as claimed in any of claims 23 to 25. 27. A high throughput method of transforming a population of protoplasts, the method comprising: (a) transforming the protoplasts with one or more heterologous polynucleotides by electroporation; (b) transferring the transformed protoplasts to an incubation buffer comprising using a multi-step buffer transfer process; and (c) incubating the transformed protoplasts in the incubation buffer. 28. A method as claimed in claim 27 which is an automated process using a liquid handling robot. 29. A method as claimed in any of claims any of claims 27 or 28 wherein the protoplasts are electroporated at an electroporation voltage of from 330 to 370 V/cm. 30. A method as claimed in any of claims 27 to 29, wherein at least 667 protoplasts per 횓쩌횓혲 are electroporated; optionally at least 1000 per 횓쩌횓혲. 31. A method as claimed in any of claims 27 to 30, wherein prior to the addition of the incubation buffer to a receptacle, the receptacle is coated with a solution comprising a protein-containing composition to reduce or prevent adhesion of the protoplasts to the receptacle surface. 32. A method as claimed in any of claims 27 to 31 wherein in step (b) a receptacle is coated with a protein-containing composition to reduce or prevent adhesion of the protoplasts to the receptacle surface, a portion of the incubation buffer is then added to the receptacle, the transformed protoplasts are added to said portion of incubation buffer, and the reminding volume of total volume of incubation buffer is then added to the receptacle. 33. A method as claimed in any of claims 27 to 32, wherein the incubation buffer comprises bovine serum albumin (BSA); optionally 0.5% (w/v) BSA. 34. A method as claimed in any of claims 27 to 33, wherein step (h) of the method comprises incubating the protoplasts in darkness; optionally for a period of 18 hours. 35. A method as claimed in any of claims 27 to 34 wherein after step (h) the protoplasts are lysed and then expression of a reporter nucleic acid is measured, preferably based upon fluorescence or luminescence. 36. A automated system for the implementation of a high-throughput method for protoplast transformation, the system comprising; (i) a liquid handling robot; (ii) a population of plant protoplasts; (iii) a plurality of heterologous polynucleotides, wherein the heterologous polynucleotides comprise a library of promoter, wherein each member of the library is operably linked to a reporter nucleic acid; (iv) at least one multi-well plate; and (v) a source of suitable reagents and buffers; wherein the system is configured to carry out the method any one of claims 1 to 24.
